behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watch behavior (recursive/subdirectory event delivery), which often differs by OS/watch backend. In-process mocks can hide missed subdirectory events.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a nested subdirectory, write a file there, and assert one transformed output appears in the output directory without manual re-scan.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Primary risk is module handoff between event intake, dedup window state, and pipeline dispatch. No real OS/network/subprocess is required to reproduce this specific failure. Rejected System because behavior text is about request dedup semantics, not OS-specific watcher behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Wire event-ingest module + dedup store + pipeline dispatcher in-process; submit two requests for identical path within the configured window and assert dispatcher is called once, then again after window expiry.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on OS-emitted duplicate events. Mocking event streams can miss real watcher timing/coalescing quirks.
behaviors.test_description: On a real filesystem, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple watcher events (create+modify/flush), capture daemon processing count, and assert exactly one pipeline execution for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Directory deletion/recreation invalidates real OS watch handles; this failure appears at OS watcher lifecycle boundaries, not pure in-process logic.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real temp watch directory, delete the directory, recreate it at same path, then add a new file and assert it is processed without restarting daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering correctness depends on real filesystem timestamp semantics/resolution and concurrent arrival timing, which in-process fakes can idealize. Rejected Integration because behavior text anchors order to creation timestamps from filesystem metadata.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files near-simultaneously on real filesystem with controlled creation times, run daemon, and assert processing order matches observed file creation timestamps.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This is a real filesystem timing/IO-completion condition (partial writes, flush timing, rename/close semantics). Mocks often hide incomplete-write races.
behaviors.test_description: Start a slow/streamed write to a file in watched dir (multiple chunks with delay), verify daemon does not process during write, then verify exactly one processing occurs after write completion.
